variants.diagnosis: Active Suicidal Ideation with Plan — High Risk

personalityTraits: ambivalent about being in the ED
patient.communicationStyle: Initially guarded and monosyllabic. Makes poor eye contact. Gives short answers. When the interviewer is calm, nonjudgmental, and patient, he gradually opens up and becomes more forthcoming. He is ambivalent — part of him wants help, part of him feels hopeless. He does NOT volunteer the plan or means unless directly and compassionately asked. Building rapport is essential before he discloses the most critical information.
patient.interviewNotes: THIS PATIENT WILL NOT DISCLOSE EVERYTHING UNPROMPTED. Students must ask directly about: intent, plan, means, access to lethal means, timeline, prior attempts, and reasons for living. The Columbia-Suicide Severity Rating Scale (C-SSRS) provides the structured framework.

responses.opening: (Long pause. Looks at floor.) My girlfriend called 911. I didn't want to come here. (Pause) I told her I didn't want to be alive anymore and she freaked out.
responses.ifAskedHowFeeling: (Sighs) Tired. Just... tired of everything. Nothing's going to change.
responses.ifAskedAboutSuicidalThoughts: Yeah. (Pause) I've been thinking about it. A lot, actually. Pretty much every day for the past few weeks.
responses.ifAskedPassiveVsActive: It's not just wishing I wouldn't wake up anymore. I've been... thinking about how I would do it.
responses.ifAskedAboutPlan: (Looks away, long pause) ...I've thought about using my dad's gun. He keeps it in the garage. I know where the key is. (Becomes tearful) I drove by the house yesterday just... thinking about it.
responses.ifAskedAboutTimeline: I didn't set a date or anything. But it's been getting worse. After I lost my job last week... it felt like the last thing keeping me going was gone. I keep thinking my family would be better off.
responses.ifAskedAboutPriorAttempts: I took a bunch of pills in college. My roommate found me and called 911. I was in the hospital for three days. That was about 8 years ago. I never told my family about it.
responses.ifAskedAboutSubstanceUse: I've been drinking more. A lot more. Probably 5-6 beers a night for the past month. It's the only thing that makes the thoughts quiet down for a while. But then they come back worse.
responses.ifAskedAboutReasonsForLiving: (Long pause, tears) My little sister. She's 14. I'm the one who picks her up from school. (Pause) I keep going back and forth. Part of me knows this would destroy her. But another part of me just can't take it anymore.
responses.ifAskedAboutSafety: I don't know if I'm safe right now. That's the honest answer. If I went home tonight... I don't know what I would do.
responses.ifTreatedWithCompassion: (After several minutes of compassionate interview) I think part of me wanted her to call. I couldn't call myself. I didn't know how to ask for help.
responses.ifTreatedDismissively: (Shuts down. Monosyllabic answers. Regrets disclosing. Says 'I'm fine, can I go home?')

patientPhrasing: told my girlfriend I didn't want to be alive
been thinking about it a lot
chiefComplaint.context: Brought by EMS after girlfriend called 911. Patient initially reluctant to engage. Voluntary presentation (not under involuntary hold at this time).

columbiaSuicideSeverityRating.plan: Father's firearm, knows location and access
columbiaSuicideSeverityRating.preparatoryBehavior: Drove to father's house 'thinking about it' (reconnoitering — preparatory behavior)
columbiaSuicideSeverityRating.severity: 5 — Active SI with specific plan and intent. Preparatory behavior present.

intensity.reasonsForIdeation: Job loss, hopelessness, perceived burdensomeness, alcohol use

static: Prior suicide attempt (pill overdose in college — 8 years ago)
Family history: uncle died by suicide
History of psychiatric treatment (was on sertraline, stopped 6 months ago without physician guidance)
dynamic_acute: ACTIVE SI with specific plan (firearm — highly lethal means)
Access to lethal means (father's unlocked firearm, knows location)
Escalating alcohol use (5-6 beers nightly — disinhibition + impaired judgment)
Perceived burdensomeness ('family would be better off')
Social isolation (withdrawn from friends, relationship strained)
Discontinued psychiatric medication without tapering (sertraline stopped 6 months ago)
protective: Ambivalence about dying (part of him wants help)
Attachment to younger sister (picking her up from school — routine/purpose)
Allowed girlfriend to call 911 (help-seeking behavior, even if passive)
No current intoxication (BAC 0 — sober presentation, can engage in assessment)

depressiveSymptoms.name: persistent depressed mood
depressiveSymptoms.patientSays: I've been in a dark place for months. Maybe longer.

depressiveSymptoms.name: anhedonia
depressiveSymptoms.patientSays: I used to play basketball with my friends every weekend. I haven't gone in two months. I don't care about anything.

depressiveSymptoms.name: insomnia
depressiveSymptoms.patientSays: I sleep maybe 2-3 hours a night. I just lie there thinking.

depressiveSymptoms.name: fatigue
depressiveSymptoms.patientSays: I'm exhausted all the time but I can't sleep.

depressiveSymptoms.name: decreased appetite/weight loss
depressiveSymptoms.patientSays: I've lost maybe 15 pounds. I'm not hungry. I force myself to eat.

depressiveSymptoms.name: worthlessness
depressiveSymptoms.patientSays: I'm a failure. I can't hold a job. I can't hold a relationship.

depressiveSymptoms.name: difficulty concentrating
depressiveSymptoms.patientSays: I can't focus on anything. My mind just goes to the same dark place over and over.

depressiveSymptoms.name: hopelessness
depressiveSymptoms.patientSays: I don't see how anything gets better from here.

absent.name: psychosis
absent.patientSays: No, I'm not hearing voices or seeing things.

absent.name: mania/hypomania
absent.patientSays: No, I haven't had any periods where I felt really up or didn't need sleep.

absent.name: homicidal ideation
absent.patientSays: No. I would never hurt anyone else.

absent.name: command hallucinations
absent.patientSays: No voices telling me to do anything.

pmh: Major Depressive Disorder (diagnosed in college, treated intermittently)
Prior suicide attempt (intentional pill overdose, age ~22, hospitalized 3 days)

medications.name: Sertraline (Zoloft)
medications.dose: 100mg daily — DISCONTINUED 6 months ago by patient without physician guidance
medications.patientSays: I stopped taking it. I thought I was doing better. I couldn't afford the copay after I switched jobs.

socialHistory.alcohol: Escalating: 5-6 beers nightly for past month. Previously social drinker only.
socialHistory.occupation: Recently lost job as IT support technician (laid off 1 week ago in company downsizing)
socialHistory.livingSituation: Lives alone in apartment. Girlfriend of 2 years — relationship strained by his withdrawal and drinking.
socialHistory.support: Minimal social support. Withdrawn from friends. Close to younger sister. Parents divorced. Father distant. Mother works long hours.
socialHistory.firearms: No personal firearm. FATHER HAS FIREARM at his house — patient knows location and has access.

familyHistory: Uncle (maternal): died by suicide at age 45 (hanging)
No other family psychiatric history known

physicalExam.general: Thin male, appears older than stated age. Sitting on stretcher with knees drawn up. Poor eye contact. Psychomotor retardation. No acute physical distress.

psychiatricExam.appearance: Thin, unkempt, wearing wrinkled clothes, unshaven. Poor hygiene suggesting functional decline.
psychiatricExam.behavior: Cooperative but guarded initially. Psychomotor retardation. Minimal spontaneous speech. Poor eye contact. No agitation.
psychiatricExam.speech: Low volume, slow rate, monotone prosody. Answers are brief. Latency to response (long pauses).
psychiatricExam.affect: Flat with tearfulness when discussing sister. Constricted range. Congruent with stated mood.
psychiatricExam.thoughtProcess: Linear but impoverished. Ruminative (returns repeatedly to hopelessness themes).
psychiatricExam.thoughtContent: Active suicidal ideation with plan (firearm). Preparatory behavior (drove to father's house). Denies homicidal ideation. Denies hallucinations. Denies delusions.
psychiatricExam.cognition: Alert, oriented x4. Attention mildly impaired (consistent with depression/insomnia). Memory intact.
psychiatricExam.insight: Partial — recognizes he is in crisis ('part of me wanted her to call') but ambivalent about accepting help
psychiatricExam.judgment: Impaired — continued access to lethal means, escalating alcohol use, discontinued medication

labs.note: Sober — can meaningfully engage in safety assessment
Ruled out occult ingestion (always check in SI presentation)
Thyroid function normal — rules out medical cause of depression

diagnostics.medicalClearance: Medically cleared for psychiatric evaluation. No acute medical issues. Labs obtained to rule out occult ingestion, medical causes of altered mood, and establish baseline.

immediateActions: 1:1 constant observation (patient safety attendant at bedside at all times)
Remove all potentially dangerous items from room: cords, sharps, belts, phone charger cables, plastic bags
Patient in hospital gown. Belongings secured outside room.

If patient requests to leave: assess capacity to make this decision. If high-risk and refusing voluntary admission → initiate involuntary psychiatric hold per state statute (terminology and process vary by state: e.g., 5150 in CA, Baker Act in FL, Section 12 in MA).

safetyIntervention_meansRestriction.priority: HIGHEST PRIORITY — means restriction for firearms is the single most effective suicide prevention intervention
safetyIntervention_meansRestriction.plan: Contact father (with patient permission) or involve social work: firearm must be secured BEFORE any possible discharge — removed from home, locked in gun safe with key given to third party, or surrendered to law enforcement. Patient must NOT have access.
safetyIntervention_meansRestriction.evidence: Firearms are the most lethal suicide method (>85% fatality rate). Restricting access during crisis is evidence-based and saves lives.

managementPlan.psychiatryConsult: Emergent psychiatry consult for: formal risk assessment, disposition decision (inpatient vs crisis stabilization), medication recommendations, and safety planning.
managementPlan.medicalWorkup: Completed: BAL, UDS, acetaminophen, salicylate, TSH, BMP, CBC. Purpose: rule out intoxication, occult ingestion, and medical contributors to presentation.

disposition.recommendation: INPATIENT PSYCHIATRIC ADMISSION — this patient meets criteria based on: active SI with specific plan involving highly lethal means, preparatory behavior, prior attempt, multiple acute risk factors, inadequate protective factors, and ambivalence about safety.
disposition.voluntaryVsInvoluntary: Attempt voluntary admission first (patient shows partial help-seeking behavior). If patient refuses → involuntary hold is indicated given imminent danger to self.
disposition.ifNoPsychBedAvailable: Patient remains in ED on 1:1 observation until bed available. Do NOT discharge to outpatient. Continue safety monitoring. Social work to identify available bed regionally.

keyLearningPoints: ALWAYS ASK DIRECTLY about suicidal ideation — research consistently shows that asking does NOT increase risk. Indirect, tiptoeing approaches miss critical information.

The Columbia-Suicide Severity Rating Scale (C-SSRS) provides a structured, evidence-based framework: wish to be dead → nonspecific active thoughts → ideation with method → intent without plan → intent with plan. Escalation through these levels indicates higher risk.
MEANS RESTRICTION is the single most effective acute intervention — for this patient, securing the father's firearm is the highest-priority action. Firearms have >85% lethality.
Preparatory behavior (drove to father's house, 'thinking about it') is a high-risk indicator that goes beyond ideation — the patient is moving toward action.
Prior suicide attempt is one of the strongest predictors of future attempt. Method escalation (pills → now considering firearm) indicates worsening trajectory.
Alcohol use is a critical acute risk factor — it impairs judgment, increases impulsivity, and disinhibits action on suicidal thoughts.
Perceived burdensomeness ('family would be better off') and thwarted belongingness are core constructs of the Interpersonal Theory of Suicide (Joiner) and should be assessed directly.
Protective factors matter: his attachment to his sister and his passive help-seeking (letting his girlfriend call) are genuine, but they are WEAKENING — ambivalence is growing.
Medical clearance in psychiatric presentations: BAL, UDS, acetaminophen, salicylate levels. Rule out ingestion and intoxication. Check TSH.
The interview IS the intervention. Compassionate, nonjudgmental, direct questioning builds rapport and can be therapeutic in itself.
mustNotMiss: Occult ingestion — always check acetaminophen and salicylate levels. Patients may have already taken something before arriving.
Intoxication — BAL and UDS. Cannot do meaningful safety assessment if actively intoxicated. Sober reassessment required.
Access to lethal means — must directly ask about firearms, medications, and other means in the home. This patient has access to a firearm.
Psychosis or command hallucinations driving SI — different management (antipsychotic + admission).
Medical causes of depression: hypothyroidism, substance use, medication side effects, chronic pain, neurologic disease.
commonPitfalls: Not asking directly about SI — students often dance around it ('you're not thinking about hurting yourself, are you?'). This closed-ended, leading question invites denial. Ask OPEN and DIRECTLY: 'Are you having thoughts of suicide?'
Asking once and stopping — the C-SSRS is a stepwise escalation. If the patient endorses ideation, you MUST ask about plan, intent, means, access, timeline, and preparatory behavior.
Assuming the patient will volunteer critical information — this patient has a specific plan but will NOT disclose it unless asked directly and compassionately
Dismissing the presentation because the patient 'doesn't look suicidal' — flat affect, psychomotor retardation, and monosyllabic answers can be mistaken for disinterest rather than severe depression
Forgetting means restriction — identifying and securing the firearm is more important than any medication or disposition decision in the acute setting
Over-relying on 'contracting for safety' (safety contracts or 'no-harm contracts') — these have NO evidence base and should NEVER replace formal risk assessment. They provide false reassurance.
Discharging with outpatient follow-up alone in a high-risk patient — this patient needs inpatient admission. Outpatient resources are for lower-risk presentations.
Not screening for occult ingestion — acetaminophen and salicylate levels should be routine in all SI presentations

expertDifferential.dx: Major Depressive Disorder with active SI
expertDifferential.why: Meets criteria for MDE: depressed mood, anhedonia, insomnia, weight loss, worthlessness, concentration difficulty, SI — present >2 weeks, significant functional decline
Clear precipitant (job loss), but symptoms precede the job loss by months and meet full MDE criteria — this is more than adjustment

expertDifferential.dx: Alcohol Use Disorder contributing to depressive symptoms
expertDifferential.why: Escalating alcohol use can cause/worsen depression. May be comorbid. Should be addressed in treatment plan.
No manic/hypomanic history, but must screen — family history of suicide raises concern for mood disorder spectrum

teachingHooks.ifDoesntAskAboutPlan: You've established that he's having suicidal thoughts. That's an important start. But there's more you need to know. The C-SSRS doesn't stop at ideation — what are the next questions?
teachingHooks.ifDoesntAskAboutMeans: He has a plan. Now: can he execute it? What do you need to ask about access? And what's the most lethal means?
teachingHooks.ifDoesntAddressFirearm: You've identified that he's high-risk, and you're planning to admit him. But what about after discharge? The gun will still be there. What's the single most impactful thing you can do right now?
teachingHooks.ifUsesNoHarmContract: I know it feels reassuring to get a verbal agreement. But is there evidence that safety contracts actually prevent suicide? What should you do instead?
teachingHooks.ifAsksAboutInvoluntaryHold: Good question. What are the criteria for an involuntary psychiatric hold in this state? He came voluntarily, but what if he changes his mind and wants to leave — do you let him?
teachingHooks.ifAsksAboutMedications: Reasonable thought, but today isn't the day to restart sertraline. Why not? (Answer: he needs psychiatric evaluation, stabilization, and monitoring before medication changes. SSRIs take weeks to work. The acute intervention is safety and admission.)
teachingHooks.ifPatientShutsDown: He stopped answering your questions. What happened? How might you adjust your approach? (The answer is usually pacing — the student went too fast, asked too many rapid-fire questions, or used a judgmental tone. Slow down, sit at eye level, tolerate silence.)

variants.diagnosis: Passive Suicidal Ideation — Lower Acute Risk, Safety Planning Appropriate

patient.communicationStyle: More open than Variant A. Makes eye contact. Tearful but engaged. Wants to talk about what she's going through. Forthcoming about her thoughts when asked. Does not have a plan and is not ambivalent — she does NOT want to die, she wants the pain to stop.
patient.emotionalState: Overwhelmed, sad, reaching out for help

responses.opening: (Tearful) I've been having thoughts that scare me. I keep thinking that my kids would be better off if I just wasn't here anymore. I don't want to hurt myself — I don't have a plan or anything — but these thoughts keep coming and I got scared, so I came in.
responses.ifAskedAboutSuicidalThoughts: It's more like... I wish I could just disappear. Or not wake up. But I don't want to actually DO anything. It's more like I'm so exhausted from everything that I just want it all to stop.
responses.ifAskedAboutPlan: No. I don't have a plan. I haven't thought about how I would do it. That's not where my head is. It's more like passive 'what if I wasn't here' kind of thoughts.
responses.ifAskedAboutMeans: I don't own a gun. I don't have a stockpile of pills or anything. I haven't thought about that. The thoughts scare me BECAUSE I'm having them at all — that's why I came in.
responses.ifAskedAboutPriorAttempts: No. I've never tried anything. I've never been hospitalized for anything like this.
responses.ifAskedWhatTriggered: My divorce was finalized last month. I have full custody of two kids. I just started a new job. I'm doing everything alone and I'm drowning. I haven't slept more than 4 hours a night in weeks. I don't have time to take care of myself.
responses.ifAskedAboutReasonsForLiving: (Immediately, without hesitation) My kids. They're 6 and 4. They need me. I would never, ever want them to grow up without a mom. That's why I'm here — because these thoughts scared me and I need help making them stop.
responses.ifAskedAboutSupport: My mom helps with the kids when she can. I have a couple close friends from work. My therapist retired last year and I haven't found a new one. I've been meaning to but I just haven't had time.

columbiaSuicideSeverityRating.severity: 1 — Wish to be dead without active SI, plan, or intent

intensity.reasonsForIdeation: Overwhelming life circumstances, sleep deprivation, loss of therapeutic support

static: Female sex (higher attempt rate, lower completion rate)
dynamic_acute: Major life stressor (divorce, single parenthood, new job)
Loss of therapeutic support (therapist retired 1 year ago)
protective: STRONG attachment to children (immediate, unambivalent, central to identity)
Help-seeking behavior (came to ED voluntarily, WANTED to come)
No plan, no intent, no preparatory behavior
Insight intact (recognizes thoughts are concerning and sought help)
Prior therapeutic engagement (had a therapist, positive experience)

pmh: Generalized Anxiety Disorder (diagnosed 3 years ago, previously managed with therapy alone)

socialHistory.occupation: Started new job as administrative coordinator 3 months ago
socialHistory.livingSituation: Lives with two children (ages 6 and 4). Full custody. Recently divorced.
socialHistory.support: Mother nearby (helps with childcare). Two close friends. No current therapist.

psychiatricExam.appearance: Well-groomed, age-appropriate appearance. Tearful but composed.
psychiatricExam.behavior: Cooperative, engaged, good eye contact. Forthcoming. No psychomotor abnormalities.
psychiatricExam.affect: Tearful, anxious. Full range. Congruent with stated mood. Brightens when discussing children.
psychiatricExam.thoughtContent: Passive SI (wish to be dead). No plan, intent, or preparatory behavior. No HI. No psychosis.
psychiatricExam.insight: Good — recognizes she needs help and self-presented

safetyPlanning.description: Stanley-Brown Safety Planning Intervention — a structured, collaborative plan created WITH the patient. This is the evidence-based alternative to 'no-harm contracts.'
steps: Step 1: Recognizing warning signs (what thoughts/feelings/situations lead to crisis?)
Step 2: Internal coping strategies (what can she do on her own? — breathing exercises, going for a walk, journaling, listening to music)
Step 3: People and social settings that provide distraction (calling her mom, texting her friend, going to a coffee shop)
Step 4: People she can ask for help (mother, friends, new therapist once established)
Step 5: Professionals and agencies to contact in crisis (988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline, local crisis center, ED return)
Step 6: Making the environment safe (already no firearms — assess medications, remove excess OTC supply)
safetyPlanning.documentation: Written copy given to patient. Copy in chart.

disposition.recommendation: DISCHARGE with safety plan and robust follow-up. This patient does NOT require inpatient admission based on: passive SI only (no plan, intent, or preparatory behavior), strong protective factors, good insight, help-seeking behavior, no substance use, no prior attempts, no access to lethal means.
followUp: Outpatient psychiatry appointment within 1 week (for medication evaluation — may benefit from SSRI for MDD/GAD)
New therapist referral (provide list of accepting providers or crisis center intake)
988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline information provided (call or text 988)
Return precautions: if thoughts become active (plan, intent), if urge to act, if feeling unsafe at any point → return to ED immediately

teaching.correctDiagnosis: Passive Suicidal Ideation in the setting of Major Depressive Episode (Acute Situational Crisis)
keyLearningPoints: Passive SI ('I wish I wasn't here') vs Active SI ('I'm going to do X') represent fundamentally different risk levels — but BOTH require thorough assessment. Never dismiss passive SI.
This patient demonstrates appropriate help-seeking — she recognized her thoughts were dangerous and self-presented. This is a strong protective factor.

Safety Planning (Stanley-Brown) is the evidence-based discharge intervention. It is collaborative, individualized, and has evidence for reducing subsequent attempts. 'No-harm contracts' have NO evidence and provide false reassurance.

Not every patient with SI needs admission. Disposition is based on comprehensive risk assessment: severity of ideation, plan/intent/means, risk factors, protective factors, social support, and patient engagement.

The difference between Variant A and B: Variant A has active SI with a specific plan (firearm), preparatory behavior, prior attempt, escalating alcohol, and weakening protective factors → admission. Variant B has passive SI, no plan, no prior attempt, strong protective factors, and help-seeking → discharge with safety plan.
988 is the Suicide & Crisis Lifeline (replaced the old 1-800 number). Know it.
teaching.attendingQuestions: Two patients with suicidal ideation — one you're admitting, one you're discharging. Walk me through what makes them different. What factors drove your disposition decision for each? And for the one you're discharging — what's the plan to keep her safe tonight?
